raw:
ORRBEAM - New Design Goals

- I want orrbeam to be able to connect to many different nodes. Each node should have its own list of known connections.

- I want to somehow utilize this to allow two players to interact with one instance of a game simultaneously. this is achieved by the host allowing the remote user to control the host computer in parallel with the host.

    - How do you handle two keyboards? two mice?
    - The primary audience here is the emulation crowd. I want to be able to play emulated couch co-op games with friends.
    - How many instances can we connect at once?
    - if its too much processing work can we use my server as a bridge to increase concurrent users?
optimized:
### OPT-001: Multi-node connection registry
Extend orrbeam to support connecting to multiple named nodes. Each node maintains its own persistent list of known peer connections (address, identity, last-seen). Provide add/remove/list operations and persist the registry across restarts.

Source: "I want orrbeam to be able to connect to many different nodes. Each node should have its own list of known connections."

### OPT-002: Shared-control co-op session mode
Implement a session mode where a host machine permits one or more remote users to control the host in parallel with the host's local user, enabling two+ players to interact with a single running game instance simultaneously. Primary target: emulator couch co-op. Session must multiplex input from all connected participants into the host OS in real time.

Source: "I want to somehow utilize this to allow two players to interact with one instance of a game simultaneously... by the host allowing the remote user to control the host computer in parallel with the host." + "The primary audience here is the emulation crowd. I want to be able to play emulated couch co-op games with friends."

### OPT-003: Multi-input device arbitration (design question)
Design and document how shared-control mode handles multiple simultaneous keyboards and mice from host + remote participants. Research and decide: virtual input device per participant (e.g. uinput on Linux), input routing to specific emulator player slots, conflict resolution when two users hit the same key, and how the host OS distinguishes devices. Deliverable: design doc + implementation. Acceptance: two remote users + host can each drive a distinct emulator player slot without input collisions.

Source: "How do you handle two keyboards? two mice?"

### OPT-004: Concurrent participant capacity (design question)
Determine and document the maximum number of simultaneous remote participants a single host can support in shared-control mode. Benchmark CPU, GPU encode, network bandwidth, and input latency as participant count scales. Deliverable: measured capacity curve + recommended default cap + hard limit in config.

Source: "How many instances can we connect at once?"

### OPT-005: Server-bridge relay mode (design question)
Investigate using the user's server as a relay/bridge to offload encoding or fan-out work from the host, raising the concurrent participant ceiling beyond what the host alone can sustain. Evaluate: SFU-style video fan-out, input aggregation on the bridge, added latency cost, bandwidth topology (host→bridge→participants vs mesh). Deliverable: feasibility report + prototype if viable. Acceptance: document crossover point where bridge mode outperforms direct host-to-participant mode.

Source: "if its too much processing work can we use my server as a bridge to increase concurrent users?"
